items.prompt: A problem asks for the minimum capacity that makes a schedule feasible. Which candidate domain should binary search operate on?

options.text: An ordered range of possible capacity values.
Only the indices of input jobs.
The two values true and false.
Possible numbers of loop iterations.

feedback.reason: Binary search on answer searches the value being optimized.
feedback.details: The input provides data for the feasibility predicate, while candidate capacities form the ordered answer domain.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.job_indices: The optimized quantity is capacity, not a job position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.result_booleans: The predicate result guides search but is not the answer domain.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.loop_counts: Iteration count is an implementation consequence.

items.prompt: For minimizing capacity, feasible(x) is false for small x and true for large x. Which result should be returned?

options.text: The first feasible candidate.
The last feasible candidate.
The first infeasible candidate.
Any feasible candidate.

feedback.reason: Minimization over false-to-true feasibility is a first-true boundary problem.
feedback.details: The search retains a feasible midpoint while checking whether a smaller feasible candidate exists.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_true: That is usually just the largest bound, not the minimum.

items.prompt: For maximizing a minimum distance, valid(d) is true for small d and false for large d. Which result should be returned?

options.text: The last valid distance.
The first valid distance.
The first invalid distance.
The maximum bound without checking feasibility.

feedback.reason: Maximization over a true-to-false predicate is a last-true boundary problem.
feedback.details: A valid midpoint allows the search to seek a larger feasible candidate.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_true: That gives a small feasible value, not the maximum.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_false: The requested answer must remain valid.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.domain_max: The largest bound may be impossible.

items.prompt: Does the optimal answer need to appear as an element of the input array?

options.text: No. It may be any candidate value in the explicitly modeled answer domain.
Yes. Binary search can inspect only values stored in the input.
Yes. Every midpoint is an input index.
It appears in the input only when the input is sorted.

feedback.reason: Answer-space search is distinct from index search.
feedback.details: For example, minimum feasible capacity may be a sum or threshold not present in the original data.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_input_only: Binary search on answer can search an integer value range.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_mid_index: The midpoint may represent a candidate answer value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_if_sorted: Sortedness does not determine answer membership.

items.prompt: What makes a lower answer bound sound?

options.text: No valid optimal answer can lie below it.
It is a convenient small number.
It always equals the minimum input value.
It is always zero.

feedback.reason: Search bounds must contain every possible optimal answer.
feedback.details: A lower bound can come from mandatory per-item requirements, mathematical limits, or contract constraints.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.looks_small: Convenience is not a correctness proof.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_always: Some answer domains begin above zero.

items.prompt: What makes an upper answer bound sound?

options.text: It is guaranteed to be at or beyond every possible optimum, often with a known feasible candidate for minimization.
It must always equal the largest input value.
Any arbitrarily large number is automatically safe and efficient.
It equals the first midpoint selected.

feedback.reason: The upper boundary must complete the enclosure of the answer domain.
feedback.details: For first-feasible minimization, a known feasible upper candidate makes the boundary contract especially clear.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.largest_input_always: Some objectives require a sum or wider bound.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.random_large: It may overflow or create an unnecessarily wide domain.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_mid: Midpoint follows from bounds; it does not justify them.

items.prompt: Why is a feasibility predicate different from the optimization objective?

options.text: The predicate answers whether one candidate works; binary search uses those decisions to locate the optimum boundary.
They are always the same numeric expression.
The predicate must return an input index.
The objective can be ignored once a predicate exists.

feedback.reason: Binary search reduces optimization to repeated decision problems.
feedback.details: The predicate must be complete enough to classify one candidate and monotonic enough to eliminate a region.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_value: A boolean decision and an optimized value serve different roles.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.predicate_returns_index: It normally returns true or false.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.objective_not_needed: The objective determines first true versus last true and the answer domain.

items.prompt: A proposed feasibility predicate returns true, false, then true again as capacity increases. What is the correct diagnosis?

options.text: The predicate or candidate model is not suitable for ordinary binary search on answer.
An upper-biased midpoint will repair it.
Running binary search twice guarantees the optimum.
Sorting the boolean results during the search will fix them.

feedback.reason: Answer-space search needs one monotonic feasibility transition.
feedback.details: The model must be revised or a different algorithm chosen.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.search_twice: Repeated use of an invalid predicate remains unsupported.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_answers: Predicate truth is semantic, not a collection to reorder.

items.prompt: A minimization predicate is true even at the lower bound. What should a correct first-feasible search be able to return?

options.text: The lower bound itself.
Always the upper bound.
The next value after the lower bound.
No answer.

feedback.reason: The search must retain endpoint candidates.
feedback.details: This all-feasible edge case exposes updates that accidentally discard the left boundary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_plus_one: The optimum may be exactly the boundary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.not_found: Feasibility at the lower bound proves an answer exists.

items.prompt: Which steps are required to model binary search on answer? Select all that apply.

options.text: Define the numeric or ordered quantity being optimized.
Derive sound lower and upper bounds containing the optimum.
Define a complete predicate for one candidate.
Prove the predicate changes in one direction.

feedback.reason: Answer-space search is correct only when domain, bounds, predicate, and transition direction are explicit.
feedback.details: Skipping any layer turns the loop into guesswork.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.derive_bounds: The search must enclose the answer.

items.prompt: Which facts can justify answer bounds for minimum processing capacity? Select all that apply.

options.text: Capacity must be at least the largest indivisible job.
The total job sum is a safe upper bound when one period may process everything.
Zero is always a valid lower bound regardless of job requirements.
The maximum machine integer is always the preferred upper bound.

feedback.reason: Bounds should come from problem semantics and known feasible or impossible limits.
feedback.details: Loose arbitrary bounds can harm safety and clarity.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_without_proof: Some jobs require positive minimum capacity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.random_int_max: It may overflow midpoint arithmetic and is unnecessarily loose.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.largest_job: A smaller capacity cannot process that job.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sum_jobs: That candidate is guaranteed sufficient under the stated model.

items.prompt: Which properties must a feasibility predicate satisfy? Select all that apply.

options.text: It fully decides whether the given candidate satisfies the constraints.
The same candidate yields the same result during one search.
Increasing candidates preserve truth or falsity in the proven direction.
It may return an unverified guess when evaluation is expensive.

feedback.reason: The predicate is the correctness oracle for answer-space search.
feedback.details: Approximate or state-dependent decisions need a different correctness framework.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.approximate_guess: An incorrect boolean can discard the optimum.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.candidate_complete: Each midpoint needs a reliable boolean classification.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.stable: Earlier eliminations rely on stable truth.

items.prompt: Which edge cases should answer-space search handle? Select all that apply.

options.text: The optimum equals the lower bound.
The optimum equals the upper bound.
No candidate is feasible when the public contract allows a not-found result.
Only a case where the first midpoint is optimal.

feedback.reason: Boundary and absent-solution cases validate the full result contract.
feedback.details: A midpoint hit is the least demanding test.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_only: This does not exercise updates or endpoints.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.upper_optimum: The other endpoint must also remain searchable.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.none_feasible: The sentinel behavior must be defined.

items.prompt: Arrange the design process for binary search on answer.

elements.text: Define the answer quantity and optimization direction.
Derive a sound ordered candidate range.
Prove monotonicity, select first-true or last-true search, and verify the result.

feedback.reason: The loop is the final step after the optimization problem has been converted into a boundary problem.
feedback.details: A correct search cannot compensate for an incorrect answer model.

items.prompt: Arrange one minimization iteration for false-to-true feasibility over a half-open candidate range.

elements.text: If feasible, retain mid by moving the right boundary to mid.
If infeasible, move the left boundary to mid + 1.

feedback.reason: A feasible midpoint may be the minimum, while an infeasible midpoint and all smaller candidates are excluded.
feedback.details: The exact code must preserve the chosen half-open interval semantics.

items.prompt: Arrange one maximization iteration for true-to-false feasibility using a progress-safe closed contract.

elements.text: If valid, set left = mid to search larger candidates.

feedback.reason: A valid midpoint remains a candidate for the maximum and shifts the search right.
feedback.details: Upper bias ensures left = mid progresses when two candidates remain.

items.prompt: Arrange the post-search verification for a minimum feasible answer x.

elements.text: Verify x lies inside the declared answer domain.
Verify x is the lower bound or feasible(x - 1) is false.
Return x as the first feasible candidate.

feedback.reason: A minimum is proven by feasibility plus predecessor infeasibility or the domain boundary.
feedback.details: This verification catches incorrect bounds and off-by-one updates.

items.prompt: The integer answer domain contains W candidates and feasible(x) costs O(n). Select total time and iterative outer-search space.
constraints: The outer search halves the answer range.

feedback.reason: Answer-space width determines predicate-call count, and input size determines each call's cost.
feedback.details: The outer loop itself stores only numeric boundaries.

items.prompt: A minimum-capacity search uses candidate range [maxJob, sumJobs], and each feasibility check scans n jobs. Select the standard time bound in terms of range width R = sumJobs - maxJob + 1.
constraints: Feasibility is false-to-true across the range.

feedback.reason: The search performs logarithmically many candidate checks, each scanning all jobs.
feedback.details: The logarithm depends on numeric answer range width, not only on the number of jobs.

items.prompt: A maximum-minimum-distance solver first sorts n positions, then each feasibility check scans them in O(n), and the answer range has width R. Select total time.
constraints: Comparison sorting costs O(n log n).
Binary search performs O(log R) feasibility checks.

time: O(n log n + n log R)
O(n log n + n log R)

time.sort_plus_search: O(n log n + n log R)

feedback.reason: Both preprocessing and answer-space search contribute to total cost.
feedback.details: Ignoring the sort understates the one-time work; ignoring predicate scans understates the search.

items.prompt: A recursive answer-space binary search uses O(log W) recursion depth, and each predicate call allocates O(n) temporary space that is released before the next call returns. Select peak auxiliary space.
constraints: One predicate evaluation is active in each recursive frame only during that frame's work.
Predicate-local O(n) storage does not accumulate across completed sibling calls.

feedback.reason: Peak memory combines one active predicate workspace with the recursion stack.
feedback.details: An iterative outer search would remove the O(log W) stack term.

items.prompt: A feasibility predicate performs O(n log n) work, and the answer range contains W integer candidates. Select total binary-search time.
constraints: The predicate is evaluated independently at each outer iteration.

feedback.reason: Binary search does not make an expensive predicate constant-time.
feedback.details: The total complexity multiplies one evaluation cost by the logarithmic number of candidate levels.
